## Linked (Open) Data

Linked Data is a way of publishing structured data so that they can be interlinked, and be processed by computer programs. This technology enables the connection and sharing of data from different (re)sources and makes it possible to formulate semantic queries that will yield richer information than traditional database searches or internet searches would do.

The term Linked **Open** Data (LOD) refers to datasets that are freely available (open content). Datasets that are publicly available can be viewed on [lod-cloud.net](http://lod-cloud.net/). This gives you an idea of the 'linkedness' of these data and illustrates why it is called 'Linked Open Data'.

In a scholarly context, Linked Open Data offer opportunities for publishing and re-using digital research output. In the field of cultural heritage, many museums and data archives provide online access to their collections and data. In the last decade many of these institutions have embarked on projects to provide their datasets as Linked Data, in order to achieve easy cross-referencing, interlinking and integration. Thus, LOD for cultural heritage and digital humanities enable large-scale digital humanities research, collaboration and aggregation.

[Linked Open Data - What is it?](https://vimeo.com/36752317) from Europeana on Vimeo.

N.B. The concept of Linked Data is closely connected to that of the [Semantic Web](http://www.cambridgesemantics.com/semantic-university/introduction-semantic-web), an approach of the internet as a web of meaningful data in stead of as a web of documents and other files.

### Main concepts

Three main concepts when working with Linked Data are URIs, RDF and SPARQL

**Uniform Resource Identifiers** (URIs) are character strings that are used to identify a web resource. For more information, see [the Wikipedia entry about URIs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uniform_Resource_Identifier).

**Resource Description Framework** (RDF) is a standard model for data interchange on the Web. With this model the characteristics of web resources can be expressed in the form of so-called *triples*, with a *subjectpredicateobject* structure. The subject describes the resource, and the predicate describes an aspect of that resource and expresses a relationship between the subject and the object. An example of such triples might be:
     <Abraham Kuyper> <is of type> <politician>  
     <Abraham Kuyper> <has birthdate> <1837-10-29>  
     <Abraham Kuyper> <is founder of> <Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam>  
     <Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam> <is located in> <Amsterdam>  
     <Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam> <has Latin name> <Universitas Libera>  
On the basis of data stored in such triples, information from various databases can be combined (linked).

**SPARQL** is a semantic query language for databases, with which data stored in RDF format can be retrieved and manipulated. In practice, this means that these data can be searched and extracted from the databases by issuing so-called SPARQL queries. The syntax used in these queries is similar to the SQL syntax that is used to query 'traditional' databases, but is adapted to the subject-predicate-object structure of the data. It allows the use of different datasets from different locations simultaneously by specifying prefixes. An example of such a query could be: *List people born in places that were part of a VOC trading route from Dutch Ships and Sailors, using both DBpedia and Dutch Ships and Sailors* (see below).

![Image BiographyNet](images/stcn.jpg)
There are various implementations of SPARQL, one of which is the *Virtuoso SPARQL Query Editor*. This editor can be used, for example, to query the RDF-version of the *Short Title Catalog of the Netherlands* (STCN), which holds bibliographical information of books published from 1540-1800. This enables researchers to create more complex queries than is possible with the web interface to the normal STCN catalog. In this way, for example, dates of publication can be combined with genre information, to find out which genres were popular in the course of time. For more information, you are referred to the web page [Zoeken in de STCN met SPARQL](https://www.kb.nl/organisatie/onderzoek-expertise/informatie-infrastructuur-diensten-voor-bibliotheken/short-title-catalogue-netherlands-stcn/zoeken-in-de-stcn-met-sparql)   ![](../lib/images/nl.png)

![Image BiographyNet](images/stcn.jpg)
![](../lib/images/nl.png)

### DBPedia: a structured version of Wikipedia

![Logo DBpedia](images/logo_dbpedia.jpg)
DBpedia is a crowd-sourced community effort to extract structured information from Wikipedia and make this information available on the Web. Information that has been entered on Wikipedia by volunteers, can be searched in a structured and machine readable way via DBpedia. Wikipedia uses categories and subjects (you can see such information e.g. in small boxes at the bottom of Wikipedia articles) and provides information like dates and places related to events and persons. DBpedia allows you to ask sophisticated queries against Wikipedia, but the DBpedia dataset can also be used to link other datasets on the Web to Wikipedia data. As such it plays an important role in many Linked Open Data projects, as can be seen in [Linking Open Data cloud diagram](http://lod-cloud.net/) mentioned above.

![Logo DBpedia](images/logo_dbpedia.jpg)

### Linked Open Data projects with a VU connection

![Image BiographyNet](images/biographynet.jpg)
[BiographyNet](http://www.biographynet.nl/)  
*BiographyNet* has created an interlinked semantic knowledge base by extracting relations between people, places, historic events and time periods based on data from biographical descriptions in the Biography Portal of the Netherlands.

![Image BiographyNet](images/biographynet.jpg)

![ILogo Rijks](images/rijks_logo.png)
[Rijksmuseum as Linked Open Data](http://datahub.io/dataset/rijksmuseum)  
The Rijksmuseum linked dataset contains over 350,000 objects, including detailed descriptions and high-quality images released under a public domain license. Also available on this site are collection and vocabulary statistics, as well as lessons learned from the process of converting the collection to Linked Data.

![ILogo Rijks](images/rijks_logo.png)

![Logo CEDAR](images/cedar_logo.jpg)
[CEDAR Linked Open Census data](https://www.cedar-project.nl/)  
This project takes Dutch census data as its starting point to build a semantic data-web of historical information. With such a web, it will be possible to answer questions such as: *What kind of patterns can be identified and interpreted as expressions of regional identity? How can patterns of changes in skills and labour be related to technological progress and patterns of geographical migration? How can changes of local and national policies in the structure of communities and individual lives be traced?*

![Logo CEDAR](images/cedar_logo.jpg)

[![Image Dutch Ships and Sailors](images/dss.jpg)](http://dutchshipsandsailors.nl/?page_id=50)
[Dutch Ships and Sailors](http://dutchshipsandsailors.nl/)  
*Dutch Ships and Sailors* provides an infrastructure for maritime historical datasets, linking correlating data through semantic web technology. It brings together datasets related to recruitment and shipping in the East-India trade (mainly 18th century) and in the shipping of the northern provinces of the Netherlands (mainly 19th century).

![Image Dutch Ships and Sailors](images/dss.jpg)

![Logo Amsterdam Museum](images/logo_amsterdam_museum.jpg)
[Amsterdam Museum Linked Open Data](http://semanticweb.cs.vu.nl/lod/am/)  
The Amsterdam Museum dataset describes more than 70.000 cultural heritage objects related to the city of Amsterdam described by the museum.

![Logo Amsterdam Museum](images/logo_amsterdam_museum.jpg)

### Tutorials

* [Linked Data: Guides and Tutorials](http://linkeddata.org/guides-and-tutorials) (From Tom Heath's web site [Linked Data - Connect Distributed Data across the Web](http://linkeddata.org/home)).
* Tutorial [Using SPARQL to access Linked Open Data](http://programminghistorian.org/lessons/graph-databases-and-SPARQL) by Matthew Lincoln  
  This tutorial explains why many cultural institutions are adopting graph databases, and how researchers can access these data though the query language called SPARQL.

### Further reading

* [What Is Linked Data?](http://www.cambridgesemantics.com/semantic-university/what-linked-data)   
  A short introductory video lecture from Manu Sporny from CambridgeSemantics.
* [Data For Dummies](https://www.erfgoedleiden.nl/educatie/open-data-blog) (Erfgoed Leiden en Omstreken)    ![](../lib/images/nl.png)   
  Linked Open Data explained in simple terms, in the context of enhancing the accessibility of digital heritage collections. [In Dutch]
* [Linked Open Data](http://www.den.nl/thema/129/)    ![](../lib/images/nl.png)   
  Theme page of the DEN Foundation (Digitaal Erfgoed Nederland / Digital Heritage Netherlands), with a brief explanation of Linked Data and links to more information and a road map for the publication of a dataset as Linked Open Data. [In Dutch]
* [*Linked Data: Evolving the Web into a Global Data Space*](http://linkeddatabook.com/book) by Tom Heath and Christian Bizer, ©2013  
  This book gives an overview of the principles of Linked Data as well as the Web of Data that has emerged through the application of these principles. The book discusses patterns for publishing Linked Data, describes deployed Linked Data applications and examines their architecture.
* [Linked Data for Digital History: Lessons Learned from Three Case Studies](http://www.victordeboer.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/04/LinkedDataforDigitalHistory_submitted.pdf) by Victor de Boer, Albert Meroño-Peñuela and Niels Ockeloen. In: *Historiografía digital: proyectos para almacenar y construir la Historia*. Mirella Romero Recio and Mª Jesús Colmenero Ruiz (eds.) Anejos de la Revista de Historiografía. Universidad Carlos III de Madrid, 2016.  
  In this paper, the authors present a number of case studies which use Linked Data principles for the representation and publication of digital history datasets. The three research projects that are presented are all collaborations between Dutch humanities researchers and computer scientists from VU University Amsterdam.

![](../lib/images/nl.png)   
Linked Open Data explained in simple terms, in the context of enhancing the accessibility of digital heritage collections. [In Dutch]
![](../lib/images/nl.png)   
Theme page of the DEN Foundation (Digitaal Erfgoed Nederland / Digital Heritage Netherlands), with a brief explanation of Linked Data and links to more information and a road map for the publication of a dataset as Linked Open Data. [In Dutch]

--- SOURCE 3: Understanding linked data and linked open data - Navigating.art ---
URL: https://www.navigating.art/articles-from-navigatingart/linked-open-data-for-art-researchers

SUMMARY:
Linked data and linked open data (LOD) are transforming how information is structured, accessed, and shared online, and they offer art historians new ways to research, publish, and foster greater collaboration across institutions. This article explains linked data and linked open data, explores their use in digital publications and museums, and discusses why it is helpful for art researchers to understand and adopt linked open data standards. For art historians, linked open data means that vast amounts of data about artworks, artists, and historical periods can be freely accessed and integrated into research, digital publications, and educational tools. For art historians and others in the digital humanities, understanding and adopting linked open data standards is beneficial for promoting interdisciplinary collaboration, ensuring long-term accessibility and preservation, and enhancing the availability and impact of research.

FULL CONTENT:
![Navigating.art](//images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/664324246220e51f0628135c/aa3dbf00-0fde-4592-864a-997ec65b288c/NA+Logo+Large+White.png?format=1500w)
![Navigating.art](//images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/664324246220e51f0628135c/aa3dbf00-0fde-4592-864a-997ec65b288c/NA+Logo+Large+White.png?format=1500w)

# Understanding linked data and linked open data: a guide for art researchers

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/664324246220e51f0628135c/2e8cbc98-4536-44ed-a46e-e83ffa23452a/Gallen-Kallela_The+Lamenting+Boat%2C+1906.jpeg)

Akseli Gallen-Kallela, [*The Lamenting Boat*](https://gallenkallela.org/catalogue/artworks/detail?title=boat&a=70-the-lamenting-boat-purren-valitus), 1906, 43 × 63 cm, tempera on canvas. Image courtesy of the [Finnish National Gallery](https://www.kansallisgalleria.fi/en/object/611596).

[Linked data and linked open data (LOD)](https://www.navigating.art/articles-from-navigatingart/glossary/#LOD) are transforming how information is structured, accessed, and shared online, and they offer art historians new ways to research, publish, and foster greater collaboration across institutions. This article explains linked data and linked open data, explores their use in digital publications and museums, and discusses why it is helpful for art researchers to understand and adopt linked open data standards.

## What is linked data?

Linked data refers to a method of connecting related [data](https://www.navigating.art/articles-from-navigatingart/glossary/#data) across different sources on the web. It requires data to be structured in such a way that it is easily interconnected and understood by machines, which allows for more efficient retrieval and integration of information from various sources.

In the context of art history, linked data allows for the connection of information related to artworks, artists, historical contexts, and more. For example, suppose various institutions have an artist's biographical record, body of work, exhibitions in which their work has been shown, and relevant scholarly articles or books published in a linkable structure. In that case, other researchers can link to that data rather than recreating it themselves. This creates a rich, interconnected web of information that is far more useful than isolated pieces of knowledge.

## What is Linked Open Data?

Linked open data (LOD) takes the principles of linked data a step further by ensuring that the data is not only interconnected but also freely available for anyone to access, use, and share. The "open" aspect of LOD refers to the commitment to making data publicly accessible without restrictions. This openness is crucial for fostering collaboration, innovation, and transparency, particularly in the academic and cultural sectors.

For art historians, linked open data means that vast amounts of data about artworks, artists, and historical periods can be freely accessed and integrated into research, digital publications, and educational tools. This promotes a more inclusive and comprehensive understanding of art history, as scholars worldwide can contribute to and benefit from the shared pool of knowledge.

## How linked data and linked open data work

Understanding how linked data and LOD work requires a closer look at the process of creating and managing these datasets. The process typically involves the following steps:

**Identifying resources:** The first step is identifying the resources you want to describe and link. In art history, these resources could include artworks, artists, exhibitions, historical periods, and related entities.

**Assigning URIs:** Each resource is then assigned a unique URI, which acts as a stable, persistent identifier. This ensures the resource can be referenced unambiguously across different datasets and platforms. ([Read more about URIs](https://op.europa.eu/en/web/webguide/uris).)

**Creating RDF triples:** Relationships between resources are described using RDF triples. For example, you might create a triple that links an artist to their artworks, or a painting to the museum that houses it. ([Read more about RDF](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Resource_Description_Framework#:~:text=The%20Resource%20Description%20Framework%20(RDF,and%20exchange%20of%20graph%20data.).)

**Using vocabularies and ontologies:** Standardized vocabularies and ontologies are important to ensure consistency and interoperability. They provide a common language for describing resources and relationships. Vocabularies like the Getty vocabularies (e.g., [the Art & Architecture Thesaurus](https://www.getty.edu/research/tools/vocabularies/aat/)) are widely used for this purpose.

**Publishing the data:** The final step is to publish the data on the web with an open license, making it freely accessible for others to use and link to. This can be done using platforms like GitHub, institutional repositories, or [dedicated LOD services](https://pro.europeana.eu/page/resources-for-sharing-data).

By following these steps, art historians can create linked open data that contributes to a global web of interconnected information, enriching the field of art history and making it more accessible to a wider audience.

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/664324246220e51f0628135c/f7b81e93-ce0f-46ac-9717-6253eb4570ac/Rijksmuseum+website.png)

## Benefits of linked open data in arts research and art institutions

For art historians and others in the digital humanities, understanding and adopting linked open data standards is beneficial for promoting interdisciplinary collaboration, ensuring long-term accessibility and preservation, and enhancing the availability and impact of research.

### Promoting interdisciplinary research

Digital art history is inherently interdisciplinary, often involving collaboration between scholars from different fields, such as art history, computer science, data science, and digital media. Linked open data standards provide a common framework that facilitates this collaboration by ensuring that [data is structured](https://www.navigating.art/articles-from-navigatingart/glossary/#Structureddata) in a way that can be easily understood and used by different disciplines.

For example, a project that combines art history with digital mapping might use LOD to link artworks with historical maps, enabling researchers to explore the geographical context of artistic movements. By adopting LOD standards, researchers can ensure that their data is [interoperable](https://www.navigating.art/articles-from-navigatingart/glossary/#Interoperable) and can be integrated into interdisciplinary research.

### Ensuring long-term accessibility and preservation

One of the key challenges in digital art history is ensuring that data remains accessible and usable over the long term. Future access depends on website hosting, program maintenance, and reliable repositories. Linked open data standards help address this challenge by promoting the use of stable, persistent identifiers (such as URIs) and standardized formats (such as RDF). This helps to ensure that data can be easily retrieved, understood, and reused in the future, even as technologies and platforms evolve. This is particularly important for preserving digital research outputs, such as digital catalogues raisonnés, [databases](https://www.navigating.art/articles-from-navigatingart/glossary#Database), and visualizations. By adopting LOD standards, researchers can work to improve accessibility and relevancy for future scholars.

### Enhancing research visibility and reuse

Adopting linked open data standards can also enhance the impact and visibility of research. By making data freely available and interconnected, researchers can reach a wider audience, including other scholars, educators, students, and the general public. Work is more likely to be found, cited, and used by others, increasing its impact and visibility.

This not only increases the visibility of the research but also fosters greater engagement and collaboration. By linking data across different sources and platforms, LOD creates a network of interconnected information that can be easily discovered through search engines, digital libraries, and other online platforms. In this way, the [reuse](https://www.navigating.art/articles-from-navigatingart/glossary#Reusable) of LOD datasets can lead to new insights and discoveries as researchers build upon and integrate existing data in novel ways.

### Supporting ethical data practices

Ethical considerations are central to digital art history and the digital humanities, particularly regarding data management and sharing. LOD standards promote ethical data practices by encouraging transparency, accountability, and responsible data sharing. Researchers can use LOD to ensure that their data is used in ways that respect the rights of individuals, communities, and cultural heritage.

For example, the flexibility of the Getty Vocabularies allows researchers to easily flag outdated language as pejorative and add updated, more accurate terms without erasing institutional history. This approach protects the data's integrity and supports more ethical and inclusive research practices.

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/664324246220e51f0628135c/7fce3973-81d3-44b1-95bb-120eb0c87e19/Bildschirmfoto+2024-09-24+um+12.19.11%E2%80%AFPM.png)

## Key organizations promoting linked open data in collections and archives

Several prominent arts, library, and archival organizations actively promote linked open data, offering resources, guidelines, and case studies to help institutions and scholars adopt LOD standards. These include:

[Getty Research Institute](https://www.getty.edu/research/tools/vocabularies/lod/): The Getty provides linked open data for its vocabularies, including the Art & Architecture Thesaurus (AAT) and the Getty Thesaurus of Geographic Names (TGN), making them accessible for research and digital projects.

[Europeana](https://pro.europeana.eu/page/linked-open-data): Europeana aggregates cultural heritage data from across Europe and offers extensive resources on using linked open data to enhance the [accessibility](https://www.navigating.art/articles-from-navigatingart/glossary/#Accessible) and [interoperability](https://www.navigating.art/articles-from-navigatingart/glossary/#Interoperable) of digital collections.

[Digital Public Library of America (DPLA)](https://pro.dp.la/projects): DPLA supports linked open data initiatives, offering tools and datasets to help institutions create more connected and accessible digital collections.

[Library of Congress](https://www.loc.gov/standards/): The Library of Congress provides guidelines and tools for implementing linked data, including its Bibliographic Framework Initiative (BIBFRAME), which aims to make bibliographic data more accessible and interoperable.

[Art Institute of Chicago](https://www.artic.edu/open-access/open-access-images): The Art Institute of Chicago has made parts of its collection available as linked open data, offering datasets and technical documentation to support scholarly research and digital projects.

[The Rijksmuseum (Netherlands)](https://www.rijksmuseum.nl/en/research/conduct-research/data/overview) The Rijksmuseum has made significant portions of its collection available as LOD, enabling users and researchers to explore connections between artworks, artists, and broader art movements. They align their data with Europeana and Wikidata to improve accessibility.

These organizations are at the forefront of the movement to make cultural heritage data more accessible and interconnected, providing valuable resources for anyone looking to implement linked open data in the arts and humanities.

## Linked data and linked open data in digital publications

Digital publications are a prime example of how linked data and LOD can be used to enhance scholarly work in art history. Traditional print publications are static and limited in scope, whereas digital publications can be dynamic, interactive, and richly interconnected through linked data.

### Enhancing content with linked data

Authors can connect their work to a vast web of related resources by integrating linked data into digital publications. For instance, a digital catalogue raisonné might include links to biographical information about artists, [metadata](https://www.navigating.art/articles-from-navigatingart/glossary#Metadata) about specific artworks, related historical documents, and scholarly articles drawn from various online databases via linked data.

This interconnected approach not only enriches the reader's experience by providing a more comprehensive understanding of the subject but also ensures that the publication remains current. As new data becomes available, it can be automatically linked to the publication, keeping it relevant.

### Improving discoverability

Linked data also improves the discoverability of digital publications. When publications are connected to a broader web of linked data, they become more easily searchable and accessible through search engines and digital libraries. This is particularly important for art historians who want their work to reach a wider audience, including scholars, students, and the general public.

Users searching for related topics can more easily find a digital publication linked to data from museum collections, bibliographic databases, and academic repositories. This interconnectedness increases the visibility and impact of the research.

### Facilitating collaboration and citation

Incorporating linked data into digital publications also facilitates collaboration among scholars. Researchers can create a more robust and interconnected body of knowledge by linking to and building upon each other's work. Moreover, linked data enables more precise and consistent citation practices, as each referenced resource is uniquely identified and can be easily accessed by others.

A researcher writing about a particular artist might link to related artworks in a museum's online collection, a database of critical essays, and a catalog of past exhibitions. Other scholars can then follow these links to explore the primary sources, build on the original research, and cite it accurately in their own work.

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/664324246220e51f0628135c/966dbe55-6316-46c8-a235-48533265ea54/Linked+open+data+at+SAAM)

## **Linked data and linked open data in museums**

Museums are among the most significant adopters of linked data and LOD, using these technologies to manage their collections, share information, and engage with the public. The shift toward linked data and LOD is transforming how museums organize their collections and interact with other institutions and audiences.

### Collection management and interoperability

Linked data [creates more possibilities for](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2543925122001115) museums to [manage their collections efficiently by connecting various pieces of information within their own databases and with external sources](https://dlib.nyu.edu/awdl/isaw/isaw-papers/7/taylor/). For example, linked data could connect an artwork's provenance with its exhibition history, conservation records, and related works in other collections.

This interconnected approach also promotes interoperability between institutions. Museums could use linked data to share information about their collections with other museums, galleries, libraries, and archives, creating a more comprehensive and accessible global network of cultural heritage. This is particularly important for collaborative projects, traveling exhibitions, and research that spans multiple collections.

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/664324246220e51f0628135c/78d19432-2a5f-46d7-8054-c6dd7a025259/American+Art+Collaborative.png)

### Enhancing visitor experience

Museums are [increasingly using linked data to enhance the visitor experience](https://npg.si.edu/linked-open-data), both in-person and online. Linked data enables museums to provide richer, more contextualized information about artworks and exhibits. For example, a visitor using a [museum's app](https://ceur-ws.org/Vol-1361/paper3.pdf) might scan a QR code next to a painting and instantly access linked data that provides biographical information about the artist, details about the painting's history, and connections to related works in other museums.

Online, linked data can power virtual exhibits, allowing users to explore collections in new ways. For example, a virtual tour of an exhibition might include links to related artworks in other museums, scholarly articles, and multimedia content, all connected through linked data. This creates a more engaging and informative experience for users, whether they are visiting the museum in person or exploring it remotely.

### Open access and public engagement

Linked open data plays a crucial role in museums' efforts to make their collections more accessible to the public. By releasing their data as LOD, museums can share their collections with a global audience, enabling researchers, educators, students, and the general public to access and use the data freely.

This openness not only supports scholarly research but also fosters public engagement with cultural heritage. For example, the Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam has made [high-resolution images of public domain objects from their collection available as LOD](https://www.rijksmuseum.nl/en/research/conduct-research/data/policy), allowing anyone to explore, download, and use the images for educational, research, or creative purposes. This open access approach democratizes access to cultural heritage and encourages a wider appreciation and understanding of art.

## Linked data to promote and protect cultural heritage

Linked data and linked open data are powerful tools that enable the creation of interconnected, accessible, and reusable datasets. LOD enhances research, collaboration, and public engagement, making it an essential component of any digital project.

Adopting LOD standards is not just about keeping up with technological trends; it’s about embracing a new way of thinking about data, research, and cultural heritage. As more institutions and scholars adopt LOD, the potential for new discoveries, insights, and connections grows, opening up exciting possibilities for the future of art history and the digital humanities.

![]()

Kiersten writes about digital art history for Navigating.art. Her Ph.D. in art history and curatorial experience help her to bridge the gap between technology and research.

URL: https://www.routledge.com/Linked-Data-for-Digital-Humanities/Nurmikko-Fuller/p/book/9781032055183?srsltid=AfmBOopJhNOlxtozXP-IP42fMhcyYTC5RiA52BL2Gof6AICINGXqQ8RW

SUMMARY:
Linked Data for Digital Humanities provides insights into how digital technologies can enrich and diversify humanities scholarship.

FULL CONTENT:
![Routledge Home](/images/routledge-logo.png)![Linked Data for Digital Humanities book cover](/img/covers/loading-image-sm.png)![Linked Data for Digital Humanities book cover](/img/covers/loading-image-sm.png)![Linked Data for Digital Humanities book cover](/img/covers/loading-image-sm.png)

1st Edition

# Linked Data for Digital Humanities

![VitalSource Logo](/images/vs.png)

## Description

*Linked Data for Digital Humanities* provides insights into how digital technologies can enrich and diversify humanities scholarship and make it pioneering in the digital age.

Written in non-specialist language, the book illustrates how information is captured, published, represented, accessed, and interpreted using computational systems and, in doing so, shows how technologies actively shape the way we understand what we encounter. Focusing as it does on underlying Web architecture and projects accessible online, the book has an inherently international focus. The interdisciplinary case study examples include bibliographic data from works published in England between 1470 and 1700; literature from ancient Iraq; jazz performances, predominantly from the USA in the 1930s; and even reach as far as an alien, fictional future. Whilst these case study examples span vast spatio-temporal distances, they all share a common thread in the use of the Linked Data information publication paradigm. Using existing computer science methods, as well as processes such as ontology development and database design, the book also includes reflections on practical considerations and offers advice about how to take institutional policies, socio-cultural sensitivities, and economic models into consideration when implementing Linked Data projects.

*Linked Data for Digital Humanities* discusses technological issues in the context of Humanities scholarship, bridging disciplines and enabling informed conversations across disciplinary boundaries. It will be of interest to humanities scholars, computer and data scientists, and library and information scientists.

Whilst these case study examples span vast spatio-temporal distances, they all share a common thread in the use of the Linked Data information publication paradigm. Using existing computer science methods, as well as processes such as ontology development and database design, the book also includes reflections on practical considerations and offers advice about how to take institutional policies, socio-cultural sensitivities, and economic models into consideration when implementing Linked Data projects.

*Linked Data for Digital Humanities* discusses technological issues in the context of Humanities scholarship, bridging disciplines and enabling informed conversations across disciplinary boundaries. It will be of interest to humanities scholars, computer and data scientists, and library and information scientists.

## Table of Contents

## Author(s)

### Biography

**Dr Terhi Nurmikko-Fuller** is a Senior Research Fellow at the Centre for Social Research & Methods at the Australian National University. Her research focuses on interdisciplinary experimentation into ways digital technologies and computational methods can be used to support and diversify research in the humanities, arts, and social sciences in general, and in relation to public culture, including Web Science, and the cultural heritage sector in particular. Terhi’s publications centre on topics related to Linked Data, but cover a range of others from the role of gamification and informal online environments in education to 3D digital models of items in museums in the UK and Australia.

**Dr Terhi Nurmikko-Fuller** is a Senior Research Fellow at the Centre for Social Research & Methods at the Australian National University. Her research focuses on interdisciplinary experimentation into ways digital technologies and computational methods can be used to support and diversify research in the humanities, arts, and social sciences in general, and in relation to public culture, including Web Science, and the cultural heritage sector in particular. Terhi’s publications centre on topics related to Linked Data, but cover a range of others from the role of gamification and informal online environments in education to 3D digital models of items in museums in the UK and Australia.

--- SOURCE 5: Linked Data - Digital Humanities Training workshop ---
URL: https://digital.humanities.ox.ac.uk/linked-data-digital-humanities-training-workshop

SUMMARY:
Her research involves the use of Linked Data and semantic technologies to support and diversity scholarship across a range of topics in the Digital Humanities.

FULL CONTENT:
![Oxford University](/sites/all/themes/custom/oxtheme/images/brandmark/svg/oxlogo-rect-border.svg)
![Oxford University](/sites/all/themes/custom/oxtheme/images/brandmark/svg/oxlogo-rect-border.svg)
![Logo](https://digital.humanities.ox.ac.uk/sites/default/files/styles/site_logo/public/styles/site_logo/public/dh/site-logo/dhox_logo1.png?itok=EDZGwFrW)
![Logo](https://digital.humanities.ox.ac.uk/sites/default/files/styles/site_logo/public/styles/site_logo/public/dh/site-logo/dhox_logo1.png?itok=EDZGwFrW)

# Linked Data - Digital Humanities Training workshop

Dr Kevin Page, Dr Terhi Nurmikko-Fuller, Dr David Weigl

![semantic web2](https://digital.humanities.ox.ac.uk/sites/default/files/styles/mt_image_medium/public/dh/images/event/semantic_web2.jpg?itok=Fa5uOE46 "Semantic web2")

Event type: Workshop

The Semantic Web can be thought of as an extension of the World Wide Web in which sufficient meaning is captured and encoded such that computers can automatically match, retrieve, and link resources across the internet that are related to each other. In a scholarly context this offers significant opportunities for publishing, referencing, and re-using digital research output. In this tutorial we first introduce the principles and technologies behind this 'Linked Data'.

In the second half of the session we will demonstrate two projects from the University of Oxford e-Research Centre in which Linked Data has been applied: a performance study of Wagner's Ring Cycle from the Transforming Musicology project; and an investigation of early English texts from EEBO-TCP and the HathiTrust in the ElEPHãT project. Attendees will participate in hands-on exercises which explore the Linked Data from these projects, and gain experience of designing their own ontologies.

This is an introductory tutorial which assumes no prior knowledge of the semantic web or linked data.

**Kevin Page** is a senior researcher at the University of Oxford e­-Research Centre. His work on web architecture and the semantic annotation and distribution of data has, through participation in several UK, EU, and international projects, been applied across a wide variety of domains including sensor networks, music information retrieval, clinical healthcare, and remote collaboration for space exploration. He is principal investigator of the Early English Print in HathiTrust (ElEPHãT) and Semantic Linking of BBC Radio (SLoBR) projects, and leads Linked Data research within the AHRC Transforming Musicology project.

**Terhi Nurmikko-Fuller** is a postdoctoral Research Associate at the University of Oxford e­-Research Centre. Her research involves the use of Linked Data and semantic technologies to support and diversity scholarship across a range of topics in the Digital Humanities.

**David M. Weigl** is a postdoctoral research associate at the University of Oxford e-Research Centre. His work involves the application of Linked Data and semantic technologies in order to enrich digital music information and facilitate access to a variety of musical data sources. His research interests revolve around music perception and cognition, and music information retrieval.

Event Link: <https://v1.bookwhen.com/humanitiesoxacuktraining-and-supp>

enriched_points.expanded_content: Linked Data is a method for structuring and publishing data online, enabling interconnections that machines can understand and process effectively.
Linked Data enhances archival research by facilitating data integration, allowing scholars to access interconnected datasets, thus yielding richer insights and fostering collaboration.
Interoperability is achieved through Linked Data, allowing diverse datasets from various sources to work seamlessly together, promoting comprehensive analysis and discovery of new connections.
